title: Fixing CSS Variable Priority with Nested Selectors

statement.markdown:
You are given a CSS string that defines some variables and applies styles to elements using these variables. However, due to incorrect priority handling, some styles are being overridden by other selectors. Your task is to fix the priority of the CSS variables so that the styles are applied correctly.

The input CSS string will contain variable definitions and selector blocks. You need to parse the input string, fix the priority of the variables, and return the corrected CSS string.

Example:

Input: `:root { --primary-color: #333; } .container { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .header { background-color: #666; }`

Output: `:root { --primary-color: #333; } .container { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .header { background-color: var(--primary-color); }`

Note that the background-color of `.container .header` should be the same as `.container`.
statement.inputFormat: A CSS string that defines variables and applies styles to elements using these variables.
statement.outputFormat: A CSS string with the priority of the variables fixed.
statement.notes: You should not remove any existing styles. Only fix the priority of the variables.

constraints.description: The number of variable definitions and selector blocks in the input CSS string.

examples.explanation: The background-color of `.container .header` should be the same as `.container`.

examples.output: :root { --primary-color: #333; } .container { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .header { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .footer { background-color: var(--primary-color); }
examples.explanation: The background-color of `.container .header` and `.container .footer` should be the same as `.container`.

hints.text: First hint text: Focus on the CSS variable priority and how it affects the styles applied to the elements.

editorial.title: Editorial title
editorial.intuition: Explanation of the correct approach: You need to parse the input CSS string, identify the variable definitions and selector blocks, and then adjust the priority of the variables accordingly.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach summary: You can use regular expressions to extract the variable definitions and selector blocks from the input CSS string, and then manually adjust the priority of the variables.
editorial.betterSolution: An alternative solution that is more efficient or elegant.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution details: You can use a CSS parser library to parse the input CSS string, identify the variable definitions and selector blocks, and then adjust the priority of the variables accordingly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Correctness proof: The optimal solution correctly fixes the priority of the CSS variables, ensuring that the styles are applied correctly.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: Input CSS string: `:root { --primary-color: #333; } .container { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .header { background-color: #666; }`. Output CSS string: `:root { --primary-color: #333; } .container { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .header { background-color: var(--primary-color); }`.
commonMistakes: Mistake 1: Not correctly parsing the input CSS string.
Mistake 2: Not adjusting the priority of the variables correctly.

testCases.output: :root { --primary-color: #333; } .container { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .header { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .container .footer { background-color: var(--primary-color); }
